author: @davidpeckham2405
text: On case hardening a metallurgist told me not to use water as the metal will take on hydrogen and hydride the metal.  This will produce a brittle metal.  I have read of one technique of using mercury in a vessel and surrounding it dry ice to reduce the temperature for the quench, that will cause a greater hardness.  I wonder though if you could take quenching oil and put it in a deep freeze for quenching to get a good hardness?  I wish I had the tools to try this.

author: @VeniceInventors
text: Another good reason for learning how to make your own tools is that you can have it and use it right away, rather than travel however far to a store, possibly having to wait for it to open, or having to wait one or more days when ordering online.

author: @ianbottom7396
text:
Good skills to have and I think you missed a relevant point on making a drill, maybe it’s something you can’t buy or is overly expensive, say for example long series drill bits or taken even further a drill that’s a metre or 2 long for drilling down through a wall for cable, in my experience you can’t buy such lengths, sure you’ll find drill extensions, they are expensive and have their own issues where making your own from rod steel has far more advantages than buying.

Another tip that I find very useful is using individual strands of heavy copper cable as a filler on steel and stainless when TIG welding, it’s very cheap, I normally buy a metre of 120mm2 earth wire and strip it for the individual strands and effectively use it as a TIG brazed joint without flux, it’s quite strong and also looks good if done for decorative or artistic purposes, also allows you to join thinner sections with less heat that would be difficult to weld

author: @OldManSparkplug
text:
I completely agree with your viewpoint.  If it's cheaper to buy, fine, but know how to make your own because if inspiration strikes in the middle of the night and you find yourself in need of a tool, making it is often much faster than waiting for a shop to open.  This happens to me regularly.

Also, please at least clamp your mini lathe down.  My boy gave me a hell of a fright last year when he knocked mine while I was working.  Wouldn't wish that on anyone.

author: @michaelsohocki1573
text: I strongly support learning to do stuff because it empowers the learner. I've been lecturing for years about the "information advantage", which enables a plumber to charge $160 for a $1 part, or a doctor $300 to diagnose a common cold. For centuries, a group with knowledge has used that advantage (literacy in the operating language is a big one) against people who do not understand and cannot control the outcomes. Today this is rampant in our culture--as people consume ready-to-go everything, forget any traditional knowledge of medicine, cooking, growing, mechanics, carpentry and so on---and are totally dependent on the hand that feeds them. I hope I'm not misquoting, but I believe it was Noam Chomsky who said "if you don't have a seat at the table, you're probably on the menu."

author: @totherarf
text:
One (mini) tip for grinding the "right" angle on your drill is to hold two nuts with one face against the other and the angle the nuts make with each other is the one you are after!
If you get good at it you can "see" them in your mind and the job is done!

It doesn't work with old square nuts though ;0)

author: @scotttovey
text:
Being able to do is much more preferable than having to rely on others.

You can easily control how you act and are towards others.
You have absolutely no control of how others act and are towards you.
As long as you have something others want,
they tend to treat you with respect and kindness.

When your at the mercy of others, others 
tend to not have the mercy towards you, 
that they demand you have towards others.
Namely, themselves.

Do not waste your time attempting to puzzle this out,
for there is absolutely no logic; in the way of hypocrites.
